title: Upper bounds for constant slope p-adic families of modular forms
speaker: John Bergdall
abstract: This talk is concerned with the radius of convergence of p-adic families of modular forms --- q-series over a p-adic disc whose specialization to certain integer points is the q-expansion of a classical Hecke eigenform of level p. Numerical experiments by Gouvêa and Mazur in the nineties predicted the general existence of such families but also suggested, in spirit, the radius of convergence in terms of an initial member. Buzzard and Calegari showed, ten years later, that the Gouvêa--Mazur prediction was false.

proof_stages.label: Finite-slope modular forms and Coleman families
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes modular forms of level Γ0(p), recalls the U_p slope range, and states the Hida–Coleman theorem producing a p-adically analytic family through a noncritical finite-slope eigenform over nearby weights.

proof_stages.label: Eigencurve interpretation of family size
proof_stages.summary: The radius question is reformulated on the Coleman–Mazur eigencurve as the problem of finding a neighborhood of the point corresponding to f on which the weight map is invertible.

proof_stages.label: Constant-slope radius and two upper bounds
proof_stages.summary: The constant-slope radius is defined by requiring both a p-adic family and constant p-adic norm of its U_p eigenvalue, and two upper bounds are stated: a log-derivative bound |a_p/a_p'| and a slope-only bound L(v_p(a_p)) asymptotic to c/h.

proof_stages.label: Maximum-modulus proof of the log-derivative bound
proof_stages.summary: After recentering the disc, the U_p eigenvalue is viewed as a power series of constant norm, and the p-adic maximum-modulus/Gauss-norm estimate gives r ≤ |a_p/a_p'|.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness tests via L-invariants and slope jumps
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains how the log derivative can be computed as an L-invariant and how jumps in the locally constant slope multiplicity d_{k,h} heuristically locate disappearing families, comparing these diagnostics with Gouvêa–Mazur phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Residual Galois representations and the Berger–Li–Zhu input
proof_stages.summary: For the second bound, modular forms are assigned local p-adic Galois representations whose mod p reductions are constant in families, and a theorem of Berger–Li–Zhu computes the inertial reduction of crystalline representations in a suitable weight-slope range.

proof_stages.label: Excluded weights and the asymptotic slope bound
proof_stages.summary: Weights k′ are chosen so that every slope-h eigenform of weight k′ has a residual representation different from that of f, forcing any constant-slope family through f to miss k′, and counting such nearby excluded weights yields r_f bounded by a quantity asymptotic to c/h.

examples.name: The p=5, weight 20, slope 9 newform computation
examples.note: The seven newforms give explicit norms for |a_p/a_p'| and, via slope-multiplicity jumps, show concretely how the first bound is close to but not always exactly sharp.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker gives the concrete p=5, weight 20, slope 9 data: seven newforms, computed norms of a_p/a_p', and compares these with jump radii to infer bounds on family sizes.

examples.name: Buzzard–Calegari counterexample to the Gouvêa–Mazur conjecture
examples.note: This counterexample shows that the naive p^{-h} local-constancy expectation must be corrected, and the log-derivative term |a_p/a_p'| explains the smaller radius in that case.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the Buzzard–Calegari counterexample to check the new bound, citing the calculable norm of a_p' as p and explaining how this shrinks the constant-slope radius enough to match the counterexample.

passes.reason: a modular-form/slope example deployed explicitly to show the Gouvêa–Mazur local-constancy conjecture fails
A standard modular-form slope example deployed explicitly by Buzzard and Calegari to refute the Gouvêa–Mazur local-constancy conjecture.

The Buzzard–Calegari example is explicitly used to disprove the Gouvêa–Mazur local-constancy conjecture, while the modular-form situation itself is not structurally sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.
